sources.sourceName: OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (answer key for odd-numbered review questions)

meta.notes: Psychology (PSBB) items scraped verbatim from OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch. 3 and Ch. 4 review-questions pages; correct answers from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), odd-numbered review questions only. Live OpenStax pages show CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 plus a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing/non-AI per PRD (mirrors psbb-001).

hints.prompt: These four terms (allele, chromosome, mutation, and epigenetic factor) all belong to which area of biology?
hints.rationale: All four options are concepts from genetics, the study of heredity and DNA.

hints.prompt: What distinguishes an epigenetic change from a change to the DNA sequence itself?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An epigenetic change alters gene expression without changing the underlying DNA base sequence

choices.label: B
choices.text: An epigenetic change permanently rewrites the order of DNA bases

choices.label: C
choices.text: An epigenetic change can only occur inside a chromosome

choices.label: D
choices.text: An epigenetic change creates a brand-new allele by deleting bases

hints.rationale: Epigenetics affects whether genes are switched on or off, not the DNA sequence, so it is not a change to the sequence itself.

hints.prompt: Which pair can be eliminated because one names a structure that packages DNA and the other names an alternate form of a gene - neither being a sudden change to DNA?
hints.rationale: A chromosome is a DNA-packaging structure and an allele is a gene variant, so neither is a sudden change to the DNA sequence.

questions.stem: A(n) ________ is a sudden, permanent change in a sequence of DNA.
questions.explanation: A mutation is a sudden, permanent change in a DNA sequence; mutations are an important source of genetic variation.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 3 Q1 = D.

hints.prompt: In genetics, what does the phrase 'gene expression' refer to?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The process by which a gene is switched on or off to produce a functional product

choices.label: B
choices.text: The physical transfer of genes between two adults

choices.label: D
choices.text: The measurement of a person's reaction time

hints.rationale: Gene expression is about genes being turned on or off, the focus of this field.

hints.prompt: Evolutionary psychology primarily studies which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: How behaviors and mental traits were shaped by natural selection over time

choices.label: B
choices.text: How individual genes are switched on and off by the environment

choices.label: C
choices.text: Employees' overall attitudes about their jobs

choices.label: D
choices.text: The chemistry of neurotransmitters at the synapse

hints.rationale: Evolutionary psychology focuses on adaptation and selection, not on gene expression, so it can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Which pair can be eliminated because one studies how people influence each other and the other studies the neural bases of behavior - neither being the study of gene expression?
hints.rationale: Social psychology (social influence) and behavioral neuroscience (neural bases of behavior) are not the study of gene expression.

questions.stem: ________ is the field of study that focuses on genes and their expression.
questions.explanation: Epigenetics studies gene expression: how genes are turned on or off, often in response to environmental factors, without altering the underlying DNA sequence.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 3 Q3 = C.

hints.prompt: A neuron is best described as which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A cell specialized to receive, integrate, and transmit information

choices.label: B
choices.text: A type of muscle fiber that contracts

choices.label: C
choices.text: A hormone secreted by a gland

choices.label: D
choices.text: A small bone located in the inner ear

hints.rationale: Neurons are the signaling cells of the nervous system.

hints.prompt: In the direction of information flow through a neuron, which structure handles the OUTPUT - sending signals toward the next neuron?
hints.rationale: Terminal buttons release neurotransmitter to send signals onward, so they are the output end, not where input is received.

hints.prompt: Which pair can be eliminated because one is the axon's fatty insulating layer and the other is the neuron's output/sending terminal - neither being where incoming signals are received?
hints.rationale: The myelin sheath insulates the axon and the terminal buttons send output, so neither receives incoming signals.

questions.stem: The ________ receive(s) incoming signals from other neurons.
questions.explanation: Dendrites are the branchlike extensions of a neuron that receive incoming signals from other neurons.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 3 Q5 = D.

hints.prompt: The four answer options (soma, myelin sheath, synaptic vesicles, and dendrites) are all components of what?
hints.rationale: All four are parts of a neuron or its synapse.

hints.prompt: What is the function of synaptic vesicles?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To store and release neurotransmitter molecules at the synapse

choices.label: B
choices.text: To insulate the axon and speed conduction

choices.label: C
choices.text: To receive incoming signals from other neurons

choices.label: D
choices.text: To generate energy for the entire body

hints.rationale: Synaptic vesicles store and release neurotransmitter; they are not an insulating structure, so they are not what breaks down in MS.

hints.prompt: Which pair consists of the neuron's cell body and its input-receiving branches (neither being the axon's insulating layer)?
hints.rationale: The soma is the cell body and dendrites receive input, so neither is the fatty insulation damaged in MS.

questions.stem: Multiple sclerosis involves a breakdown of the ________.
questions.explanation: Multiple sclerosis involves the breakdown of the myelin sheath, the fatty insulating layer around axons that speeds neural conduction.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 3 Q7 = B.

hints.prompt: The peripheral nervous system has two main branches. What is the key distinction between them?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One controls voluntary skeletal-muscle movement; the other controls involuntary internal functions

choices.label: B
choices.text: One controls the left side of the body; the other the right side

choices.label: C
choices.text: One is in the brain; the other is in the spinal cord

choices.label: D
choices.text: One uses hormones; the other uses bones

hints.rationale: The somatic branch handles voluntary movement while the autonomic branch handles involuntary functions.

hints.prompt: The autonomic nervous system is chiefly responsible for which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Involuntary functions such as heart rate, digestion, and glandular activity

choices.label: B
choices.text: Voluntary movement of skeletal muscles such as the legs

hints.rationale: The autonomic system controls involuntary internal functions, so it is not what moves your legs voluntarily.

hints.prompt: The sympathetic and parasympathetic systems are the two divisions of which larger system?
hints.rationale: Sympathetic and parasympathetic are autonomic subdivisions handling involuntary responses, so both can be eliminated for a voluntary-movement question.

questions.stem: Our ability to make our legs move as we walk across the room is controlled by the ________ nervous system.
questions.explanation: Voluntary control of skeletal muscles, such as moving your legs to walk, is governed by the somatic nervous system.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 3 Q9 = B.

hints.prompt: What is the primary function of the central nervous system?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To integrate information and coordinate activity throughout the body

choices.label: B
choices.text: To digest food in the stomach

choices.label: C
choices.text: To circulate blood to the tissues

choices.label: D
choices.text: To filter waste products from the blood

hints.rationale: The CNS is the body's integration and coordination center.

hints.prompt: The somatic and autonomic nervous systems are the two divisions of which larger system?
hints.rationale: Somatic and autonomic make up the peripheral nervous system, not the central nervous system.

hints.prompt: Which pair can be eliminated - one lists two subdivisions of the autonomic (peripheral) system and the other lists non-nervous body parts?

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'brain and spinal cord' and 'organs and glands'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'sympathetic and parasympathetic' and 'brain and spinal cord'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'somatic and autonomic' and 'brain and spinal cord'

hints.rationale: Sympathetic/parasympathetic are peripheral autonomic branches and organs/glands are not nervous tissue, so neither makes up the CNS.

questions.stem: The central nervous system is comprised of ________.
questions.explanation: The central nervous system (CNS) consists of the brain and the spinal cord; everything else is part of the peripheral nervous system.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 3 Q11 = D.

hints.prompt: In brain organization, a 'sensory relay station' is a structure that does what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Receives incoming sensory signals and routes them to the appropriate areas of the cortex

hints.rationale: A sensory relay station routes incoming sensory information onward for processing.

hints.prompt: Which sense is the exception that does NOT pass through the brain's main sensory relay before reaching the cortex?
hints.rationale: Olfaction bypasses the relay, exactly the clue given in the stem.

hints.prompt: Which pair of structures is associated mainly with emotion (especially fear) and with forming new long-term memories, respectively - not with relaying sensory information?
hints.rationale: The amygdala handles emotion and the hippocampus forms memories, so neither is the sensory relay station.

questions.stem: The ________ is a sensory relay station where all sensory information, except for smell, goes before being sent to other areas of the brain for further processing.
questions.explanation: The thalamus is the brain's sensory relay station; all sensory information except smell passes through it before being routed to the cortex.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 3 Q13 = D.

hints.prompt: The four options (EEG, MRI, PET scan, and CT scan) are all examples of what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Techniques for measuring or imaging the brain or body

hints.rationale: All four are brain/body measurement or imaging methods.

hints.prompt: What does an EEG (electroencephalogram) actually measure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The brain's electrical activity through electrodes placed on the scalp

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tissue structure using strong magnetic fields

choices.label: C
choices.text: Glucose metabolism using a radioactive tracer

hints.rationale: An EEG records electrical activity, not magnetic images, so it can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Which imaging pair relies on a radioactive tracer and on x-rays, respectively - and therefore NOT on magnetic fields?
hints.rationale: A PET scan uses a radioactive tracer and a CT scan uses x-rays, so neither uses magnetic fields.

questions.stem: A(n) ________ uses magnetic fields to create pictures of a given tissue.
questions.explanation: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) uses strong magnetic fields to produce detailed images of body tissues, including the brain.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 3 Q15 = B.

hints.prompt: The pancreas plays a central role in regulating which of the following in the body?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The growth of the long bones

hints.rationale: The pancreas is central to blood-glucose regulation.

hints.prompt: Estrogen and progesterone are hormones produced primarily by which structures?
hints.rationale: Estrogen and progesterone are ovarian sex hormones, not pancreatic, so that pair can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Which two answer pairs are made OUTSIDE the pancreas - one lists adrenal stress hormones and the other a thyroid hormone plus a pituitary hormone?
hints.rationale: Norepinephrine/epinephrine are adrenal hormones and thyroxine/oxytocin come from the thyroid and pituitary, so neither pair is pancreatic.

questions.stem: The two major hormones secreted from the pancreas are:
questions.explanation: The pancreas secretes glucagon and insulin, the two major hormones that regulate blood glucose levels.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 3 Q17 = D.

hints.prompt: Epinephrine (adrenaline) is the hormone most associated with which bodily response?

choices.label: C
choices.text: The lengthening of bones during growth

hints.rationale: Epinephrine drives the fight-or-flight stress response.

hints.prompt: The thyroid gland's main hormones primarily regulate which of the following?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The surge of adrenaline during a threat

choices.label: C
choices.text: The timing of the menstrual cycle

hints.rationale: The thyroid controls metabolism, not the release of epinephrine, so it can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: In the endocrine system, which gland is nicknamed the 'master gland' because it directs many other glands?
hints.rationale: The pituitary is the 'master gland,' so the options 'pituitary' and 'master' name the same gland and can both be eliminated as the source of epinephrine.

questions.stem: The ________ gland secretes epinephrine.
questions.explanation: The adrenal glands, located atop the kidneys, secrete epinephrine (adrenaline) as part of the body's stress (fight-or-flight) response.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 3 Q19 = A.

hints.prompt: The body's 'biological clock' is responsible for regulating which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Daily (circadian) rhythms such as the sleep-wake cycle

choices.label: C
choices.text: The oxygen level of the blood

hints.rationale: The biological clock governs circadian timing such as the sleep-wake cycle.

hints.prompt: The hippocampus is best known for its role in which function?
hints.rationale: The hippocampus is a memory structure, not the timekeeping center, so it can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Which pair is best described as the brain's sensory relay station and the endocrine 'master gland,' respectively - neither being the timekeeping center?
hints.rationale: The thalamus relays sensory input and the pituitary directs hormones, so neither is the body's biological clock.

questions.stem: The body's biological clock is located in the ________.
questions.explanation: The body's biological clock, the suprachiasmatic nucleus, is located in the hypothalamus and regulates circadian rhythms.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 4 Q1 = C.

hints.prompt: Which everyday example best fits a rhythm that repeats about once every 24 hours?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The beating of the heart each second

choices.label: C
choices.text: The yearly change of the seasons

hints.rationale: The sleep-wake cycle is the classic example of a roughly 24-hour rhythm.

hints.prompt: Biological rhythms come in many lengths (some hourly, some monthly, some yearly). What distinguishes the roughly 24-hour ones specifically?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are a specific subtype tied to the daily day-night cycle

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are the only biological rhythms that exist

choices.label: D
choices.text: They have nothing to do with sleep

hints.rationale: A roughly 24-hour rhythm is one specific subtype of biological rhythm, so the broad label 'biological' is too general.

hints.prompt: Which two of the answer choices are NOT established terms for biological rhythms?
hints.rationale: 'Rotating' and 'conscious' are not recognized labels for biological rhythms, so both can be eliminated.

questions.stem: ________ cycles occur roughly once every 24 hours.
questions.explanation: Circadian cycles are biological rhythms that recur on a roughly 24-hour cycle, such as the sleep-wake cycle.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 4 Q3 = B.

hints.prompt: Growth hormone is primarily responsible for which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Promoting physical growth and tissue repair

choices.label: B
choices.text: Regulating the level of sugar in the blood

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sensing light to set the sleep cycle

hints.rationale: Growth hormone drives physical growth and tissue repair.

hints.prompt: The pineal gland's main secretion, melatonin, is chiefly involved in what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Signaling the sleep-wake cycle in response to darkness

hints.rationale: The pineal gland releases melatonin for sleep timing, not growth hormone, so it can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Which pair of glands regulates metabolic rate and blood-glucose levels, respectively - neither secreting growth hormone?
hints.rationale: The thyroid sets metabolic rate and the pancreas manages blood glucose, so neither secretes growth hormone.

questions.stem: Growth hormone is secreted by the ________ while we sleep.
questions.explanation: Growth hormone is secreted by the pituitary gland, with much of its release occurring during deep sleep.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 4 Q5 = C.

hints.prompt: The pineal gland is best known for producing a hormone that helps regulate what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The sleep-wake cycle and other biological rhythms

choices.label: B
choices.text: The level of calcium in the blood

hints.rationale: The pineal gland's hormone regulates biological rhythms such as the sleep-wake cycle.

hints.prompt: Growth hormone differs from the pineal gland's hormone in that growth hormone is produced by the pituitary and does what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Drives body growth and tissue repair

choices.label: B
choices.text: Is released mainly in response to darkness

choices.label: C
choices.text: Regulates the ovarian cycle each month

choices.label: D
choices.text: Is manufactured inside the pineal gland

hints.rationale: Growth hormone is a pituitary hormone for growth, a different gland and role, so it can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: LH and FSH are gonadotropin hormones released by the pituitary to control which process?
hints.rationale: LH and FSH govern reproduction and come from the pituitary, not the pineal, so both can be eliminated.

questions.stem: ________ is a hormone secreted by the pineal gland that plays a role in regulating biological rhythms and immune function.
questions.explanation: Melatonin, secreted by the pineal gland, helps regulate biological rhythms (including the sleep-wake cycle) and immune function.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 4 Q7 = B.

hints.prompt: 'Slow-wave sleep' gets its name from the presence of which brain-wave pattern?
hints.rationale: Slow-wave sleep is defined by slow, high-amplitude delta waves.

hints.prompt: How does REM sleep differ from the deep NREM stages?

choices.label: A
choices.text: REM shows fast, wake-like brain activity with vivid dreaming and muscle atonia

choices.label: B
choices.text: REM has the slowest delta waves of any stage

choices.label: C
choices.text: REM is the light transitional stage as you first doze off

choices.label: D
choices.text: REM shows no brain activity whatsoever

hints.rationale: REM features fast wake-like activity, the opposite of slow-wave sleep, so it can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: NREM stage 1 and stage 2 are best characterized as which kind of sleep?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lighter, earlier stages (dozing off and light sleep)

choices.label: B
choices.text: The single deepest stage of the night

hints.rationale: Stages 1 and 2 are light sleep, not the deep slow-wave stage, so both can be eliminated.

questions.stem: ________ is(are) described as slow-wave sleep.
questions.explanation: Slow-wave sleep, dominated by low-frequency, high-amplitude delta waves, corresponds to NREM stage 3 (which in older schemes was split into stages 3 and 4).
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 4 Q9 = C (Guide answer text 'stage 3 and stage 4' reflects the older staging; option C is the current 2e wording).

hints.prompt: REM rebound refers to which phenomenon?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An increase in REM sleep following a period of REM deprivation

choices.label: B
choices.text: A permanent loss of the ability to dream

choices.label: C
choices.text: The sudden muscle paralysis seen in narcolepsy

choices.label: D
choices.text: A type of tumor that forms in the brainstem

hints.rationale: REM rebound is the increase in REM sleep after REM has been suppressed.

hints.prompt: Schizophrenia is primarily characterized by which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Psychotic symptoms such as hallucinations and delusions

choices.label: B
choices.text: Persistently low mood and loss of interest

choices.label: C
choices.text: A resting tremor and muscular rigidity

hints.rationale: Schizophrenia is a psychotic disorder, not the mood disorder classically linked to REM manipulation, so it can be set aside.

hints.prompt: A common trap is to link vivid REM dreaming to psychotic symptoms. Which listed disorder is the psychosis-related trap you should be cautious about choosing?
hints.rationale: Schizophrenia is the tempting psychosis-related trap, but the item's established link is to a mood disorder, so schizophrenia can be set aside.

questions.stem: Symptoms of ________ may be improved by REM rebound.
questions.explanation: Per the source, symptoms of depression may be affected by manipulations of REM sleep. The keyed answer is depression.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from the live OpenStax page (stem reads 'REM rebound'); the Student Solutions Guide prints the stem as 'REM deprivation'. Both editions key the answer to C = depression. Flagged for the stem-wording discrepancy.

hints.prompt: Narcolepsy is a sleep disorder whose hallmark is which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Overwhelming, uncontrollable episodes of daytime sleepiness or 'sleep attacks'

choices.label: B
choices.text: A chronic inability to fall asleep at night

choices.label: C
choices.text: Loud snoring with repeated pauses in breathing

choices.label: D
choices.text: Walking or eating while still asleep

hints.rationale: Narcolepsy's hallmark is sudden, uncontrollable daytime sleep attacks.

hints.prompt: Insomnia is best defined as which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Difficulty falling asleep or staying asleep

choices.label: B
choices.text: A sudden loss of muscle tone triggered by emotion

choices.label: C
choices.text: A device that keeps the airway open during sleep

hints.rationale: Insomnia is trouble sleeping, not a loss of muscle tone, so it can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Which pair consists of a disorder in which people physically act out their dreams and a therapeutic breathing device - neither being a loss of muscle tone?
hints.rationale: RBD is dream-enactment and CPAP is a treatment device, so neither is the emotion-triggered muscle-tone loss.

questions.stem: ________ is loss of muscle tone or control that is often associated with narcolepsy.
questions.explanation: Cataplexy is a sudden loss of muscle tone or control, often triggered by strong emotion, and is commonly associated with narcolepsy.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 4 Q13 = C.

hints.prompt: Amphetamine-like medications primarily produce which effect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased wakefulness and alertness (central nervous system stimulation)

choices.label: D
choices.text: A slowed heart rate and drowsiness

hints.rationale: Amphetamine-like drugs are stimulants that promote wakefulness.

hints.prompt: Sleep apnea is caused by what, and how is it usually treated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Repeated pauses in breathing during sleep, typically treated with a CPAP device

choices.label: B
choices.text: Emotion-triggered muscle-tone loss, treated with stimulants

choices.label: C
choices.text: Acting out dreams, treated with surgery

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sudden infant death, prevented by safe-sleep positioning

hints.rationale: Apnea is a breathing disorder treated with CPAP, not with amphetamine-like stimulants, so it can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Which pair can be eliminated because one is a cause of sudden infant death (not treated with stimulants) and the other is a REM dream-enactment disorder?
hints.rationale: SIDS is sudden infant death and RBD is dream enactment, so neither is treated with amphetamine-like stimulants.

questions.stem: The most common treatment for ________ involves the use of amphetamine-like medications.
questions.explanation: Narcolepsy is most commonly treated with amphetamine-like stimulant medications that promote wakefulness.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 4 Q15 = D.

hints.prompt: The terms in this question (withdrawal, dependence, and tolerance) all come from the study of what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: How the body and brain respond to repeated drug use

choices.label: B
choices.text: How neurons regenerate after an injury

hints.rationale: These are all concepts describing the body's response to repeated drug use.

hints.prompt: Withdrawal refers to which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The unpleasant symptoms that appear when a regular user stops taking a drug

choices.label: B
choices.text: Needing larger doses to feel the same effect

choices.label: C
choices.text: An emotional craving to keep using a drug

choices.label: D
choices.text: A neuron reabsorbing its own neurotransmitter

hints.rationale: Withdrawal is the set of symptoms on stopping a drug, not the need for larger doses, so it can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Which pair can be eliminated because one refers to an emotional craving for a drug and the other is a normal neuron process of reabsorbing neurotransmitter?
hints.rationale: Psychological dependence is craving and reuptake is neurotransmitter reabsorption, so neither is 'needing more for the same effect.'

questions.stem: ________ occurs when a drug user requires more and more of a given drug in order to experience the same effects of the drug.
questions.explanation: Tolerance occurs when a person needs increasing amounts of a drug to achieve the same effect that smaller doses once produced.

questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 4 Q17 = C.

hints.prompt: Drug 'craving' is best described as which kind of experience?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A strong emotional or motivational urge to use the drug

choices.label: B
choices.text: A physical injury to the liver from the drug

choices.label: C
choices.text: A chemical that blocks a receptor

choices.label: D
choices.text: An allergic skin reaction to the drug

hints.rationale: Craving is an emotional/motivational urge to use a drug.

hints.prompt: Physical dependence differs from an emotional craving in that physical dependence is defined by what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bodily withdrawal symptoms when the drug is stopped

choices.label: B
choices.text: A purely emotional desire with no bodily symptoms

choices.label: D
choices.text: Enhancing the activity of a receptor

hints.rationale: Physical dependence is marked by bodily withdrawal, distinguishing it from the emotional craving, so it can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Agonism and antagonism are pharmacology terms describing what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Whether a drug activates or blocks a receptor

choices.label: B
choices.text: Whether a user emotionally craves a drug

choices.label: C
choices.text: How many hours a drug's effects last

choices.label: D
choices.text: The legal status of a drug

hints.rationale: Agonism and antagonism describe activating versus blocking receptors, not craving, so both can be eliminated.

questions.stem: ________ refers to drug craving.
questions.explanation: Psychological dependence is characterized by an emotional, motivational craving for a drug, distinct from the physical symptoms of physical dependence.

questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 4 Q19 = A.

hints.prompt: What does it mean for a person to be highly 'suggestible'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They readily accept and act on ideas proposed by another person

choices.label: B
choices.text: They have an unusually strong long-term memory

choices.label: C
choices.text: They have great difficulty falling asleep

choices.label: D
choices.text: They have very fast physical reflexes

hints.rationale: Suggestibility is the tendency to readily accept another person's suggestions.

hints.prompt: Meditation is best characterized as which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A self-directed practice of focused attention or awareness to calm the mind

choices.label: B
choices.text: A state produced by following another person's spoken suggestions

choices.label: C
choices.text: A form of talk therapy that restructures negative thoughts

choices.label: D
choices.text: A surgical procedure performed on the brain

hints.rationale: Meditation is self-directed focus, not driven by another's suggestions, so it can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Which pair relies on the individual's own attention or thinking rather than on being highly open to another person's suggestions?
hints.rationale: Mindful awareness centers on one's own attention and cognitive therapy on one's own thoughts, so neither depends on suggestibility.

questions.stem: ________ is most effective in individuals that are very open to the power of suggestion.
questions.explanation: Hypnosis is most effective for people who are highly open to suggestion (high in hypnotic suggestibility).
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 4 Q21 = A.

hints.prompt: Meditation is best described as which kind of practice?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A self-directed mental practice involving focused attention or awareness

choices.label: C
choices.text: A surgical operation on the brain

hints.rationale: Meditation is a self-directed mental practice, not a drug, surgery, or vaccine.

hints.prompt: Research supports meditation as helpful for which of the following?
hints.rationale: Meditation is supported as an aid for stress, whereas it does not cure infections or heal fractures.

hints.prompt: Which option can be eliminated because meditation is not a medical treatment for a viral illness?
hints.rationale: Meditation is not a treatment for the flu, so that option can be eliminated, while the others are stress or relaxation benefits.

questions.stem: Meditation may be helpful in ________.
questions.explanation: Meditation has been linked to benefits for both pain management and stress control, so 'both a and b' is correct.
